About

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

Understanding the project, methodology, and team behind SG CPI Forecast

Project Overview

Singapore’s Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a critical barometer for policymakers, businesses, and households alike. As a highly open economy with no natural resources, Singapore’s price levels are uniquely sensitive to global commodity cycles, exchange rate movements, domestic wage growth, and housing costs.

This project develops and evaluates a suite of time-series and machine-learning forecasting models to anticipate CPI movements across headline, core, and sub-component indices — enabling sharper economic planning and policy analysis.

NoteData Currency

All models are re-estimated quarterly using the latest SingStat CPI release. Forecast vintages are archived to allow retrospective accuracy assessment.

Objectives

The project has three primary objectives:

  1. Forecast accuracy — Produce accurate 3–12 month ahead CPI forecasts for Singapore across headline and core measures.
  2. Model comparison — Rigorously benchmark classical econometric models against modern machine learning approaches.
  3. Transparency — Provide fully reproducible R code and documentation so researchers can replicate, adapt, and extend the analysis.

Methodology

Data Sources

Source Variables Used Frequency
SingStat Headline CPI, Core CPI, sub-indices Monthly
MAS SGD NEER, monetary policy statements Monthly
World Bank Global commodity price index Monthly
IEA Brent crude oil prices Monthly
MOM Singapore Wage indices, employment data Quarterly

Model Suite

We implement and compare six forecasting approaches:

Model Type Horizon Key Strengths
ARIMA Classical 3–12 months Interpretable, parsimonious baseline
SARIMA Classical 3–12 months Captures seasonal sub-index patterns
VAR Classical 3–6 months Multivariate macro linkages
XGBoost Machine Learning 1–6 months Handles non-linearity, best RMSE
LSTM Deep Learning 1–3 months Sequence modelling, long memory
Ensemble Combined 3–12 months Reduces model-specific bias

Evaluation Metrics

All models are compared using:

  • RMSE — Root Mean Squared Error (penalises large errors)
  • MAE — Mean Absolute Error (robust to outliers)
  • MASE — Mean Absolute Scaled Error (scale-independent)
  • Directional Accuracy — % of months where the sign of change is correctly predicted

Tools & Stack

This project is built entirely in R and documented with Quarto.

# Core packages used
library(tidyverse)    # Data wrangling
library(fable)        # Tidy time-series forecasting (ARIMA, ETS, VAR)
library(forecast)     # Legacy ARIMA/SARIMA tooling
library(vars)         # Vector Autoregression
library(tidymodels)   # ML framework
library(xgboost)      # Gradient boosting
library(keras)        # LSTM via TensorFlow backend
library(tsibble)      # Tidy time-series data structures
library(feasts)       # Feature extraction & visualisation
library(plotly)       # Interactive charts
TipReproducibility

All analyses use renv for package version locking. Run renv::restore() after cloning the repository to reproduce the exact environment.

Data Pipeline

Code 
flowchart LR
    A[SingStat API] --> B[Raw CPI Data]
    C[MAS / World Bank] --> B
    B --> D[Data Cleaning\n& Validation]
    D --> E[Feature Engineering]
    E --> F1[ARIMA / SARIMA]
    E --> F2[VAR]
    E --> F3[XGBoost]
    E --> F4[LSTM]
    F1 --> G[Ensemble]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Evaluation\n& Reporting]

About the Author

This project was developed as part of economic research into Singapore’s macroeconomic dynamics.

Researcher
Singapore Economic Research

Interests include applied econometrics, time-series forecasting, and data-driven policy analysis. Proficient in R, Python, and Quarto for reproducible research.

GitHub · LinkedIn · Email

Citation

If you use this work, please cite:

@misc{sgcpiforecast2024,
  title   = {Singapore CPI Forecasting: A Multi-Model Comparison},
  author  = {Your Name},
  year    = {2024},
  url     = {https://yoursite.github.io/sg-cpi-forecast}
}

License

This project is released under the MIT License. Data from SingStat is used under their terms of use.

Source Code
---
title: "About"
subtitle: "Understanding the project, methodology, and team behind SG CPI Forecast"
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
---

## Project Overview

Singapore's Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a critical barometer for policymakers, businesses, and households alike. As a highly open economy with no natural resources, Singapore's price levels are uniquely sensitive to global commodity cycles, exchange rate movements, domestic wage growth, and housing costs.

This project develops and evaluates a **suite of time-series and machine-learning forecasting models** to anticipate CPI movements across headline, core, and sub-component indices — enabling sharper economic planning and policy analysis.

::: {.callout-note}
## Data Currency
All models are re-estimated quarterly using the latest SingStat CPI release. Forecast vintages are archived to allow retrospective accuracy assessment.
:::

---

## Objectives

The project has three primary objectives:

1. **Forecast accuracy** — Produce accurate 3–12 month ahead CPI forecasts for Singapore across headline and core measures.
2. **Model comparison** — Rigorously benchmark classical econometric models against modern machine learning approaches.
3. **Transparency** — Provide fully reproducible R code and documentation so researchers can replicate, adapt, and extend the analysis.

---

## Methodology

### Data Sources

| Source | Variables Used | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| [SingStat](https://www.singstat.gov.sg/) | Headline CPI, Core CPI, sub-indices | Monthly |
| [MAS](https://www.mas.gov.sg/) | SGD NEER, monetary policy statements | Monthly |
| [World Bank](https://www.worldbank.org/) | Global commodity price index | Monthly |
| [IEA](https://www.iea.org/) | Brent crude oil prices | Monthly |
| [MOM Singapore](https://www.mom.gov.sg/) | Wage indices, employment data | Quarterly |

### Model Suite

We implement and compare six forecasting approaches:

```{r}
#| label: model-summary-table
#| echo: false

library(tibble)
library(knitr)

models <- tribble(
  ~Model,        ~Type,            ~Horizon,     ~Key_Strengths,
  "ARIMA",       "Classical",      "3–12 months","Interpretable, parsimonious baseline",
  "SARIMA",      "Classical",      "3–12 months","Captures seasonal sub-index patterns",
  "VAR",         "Classical",      "3–6 months", "Multivariate macro linkages",
  "XGBoost",     "Machine Learning","1–6 months","Handles non-linearity, best RMSE",
  "LSTM",        "Deep Learning",  "1–3 months", "Sequence modelling, long memory",
  "Ensemble",    "Combined",       "3–12 months","Reduces model-specific bias"
)

kable(models, col.names = c("Model", "Type", "Horizon", "Key Strengths"),
      align = "llll")
```

### Evaluation Metrics

All models are compared using:

- **RMSE** — Root Mean Squared Error (penalises large errors)
- **MAE** — Mean Absolute Error (robust to outliers)
- **MASE** — Mean Absolute Scaled Error (scale-independent)
- **Directional Accuracy** — % of months where the sign of change is correctly predicted

---

## Tools & Stack

This project is built entirely in **R** and documented with **Quarto**.

```r
# Core packages used
library(tidyverse)    # Data wrangling
library(fable)        # Tidy time-series forecasting (ARIMA, ETS, VAR)
library(forecast)     # Legacy ARIMA/SARIMA tooling
library(vars)         # Vector Autoregression
library(tidymodels)   # ML framework
library(xgboost)      # Gradient boosting
library(keras)        # LSTM via TensorFlow backend
library(tsibble)      # Tidy time-series data structures
library(feasts)       # Feature extraction & visualisation
library(plotly)       # Interactive charts
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Reproducibility
All analyses use `renv` for package version locking. Run `renv::restore()` after cloning the repository to reproduce the exact environment.
:::

---

## Data Pipeline

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[SingStat API] --> B[Raw CPI Data]
    C[MAS / World Bank] --> B
    B --> D[Data Cleaning\n& Validation]
    D --> E[Feature Engineering]
    E --> F1[ARIMA / SARIMA]
    E --> F2[VAR]
    E --> F3[XGBoost]
    E --> F4[LSTM]
    F1 --> G[Ensemble]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Evaluation\n& Reporting]
```

---

## About the Author

This project was developed as part of economic research into Singapore's macroeconomic dynamics.

**Researcher**  
*Singapore Economic Research*

Interests include applied econometrics, time-series forecasting, and data-driven policy analysis. Proficient in R, Python, and Quarto for reproducible research.

## Citation

If you use this work, please cite:

```bibtex
@misc{sgcpiforecast2024,
  title   = {Singapore CPI Forecasting: A Multi-Model Comparison},
  author  = {Your Name},
  year    = {2024},
  url     = {https://yoursite.github.io/sg-cpi-forecast}
}
```

## License

This project is released under the [MIT License](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT). Data from SingStat is used under their [terms of use](https://www.singstat.gov.sg/terms-of-use).

Built with R · Quarto · SingStat data · Updated quarterly
